---
title: "Python Tools for Management Research"
subtitle: "1c: Polars III"
author: "Jason T. Kiley"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: _style.css
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    controls: true
    transition: fade
    preview-links: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-copy: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
---


## Polars III

::: {.big-claim}

Now we make the same data work easier to read and easier to scale.

:::

This segment helps us scale: lazy queries and functions for readable pipelines.


## Why lazy?

Lazy Polars lets us describe the work first and run it later.

That gives Polars room to optimize the query, avoid unnecessary work, and handle larger data nicely on typical research hardware.

The [lazy API usage docs](https://docs.pola.rs/user-guide/lazy/using/){target="_blank"} have the full version.


## Why functions?

A huge chained call can work and still be hard to maintain.

::: {.scroll-code}

```python
firmyear_pipeline = (
    firmyear_source.with_columns(...)
    .rename(...)
    .sort(...)
    .join(
        lookup,
        on="name",
        how="left",
        validate="m:1",
    )
    .join(
        pl.scan_csv(DATA_DIR / "stock.csv"),
        left_on=["id_ticker", "year"],
        right_on=["tic", "yr"],
        how="left",
        validate="1:1",
    )
    .join(
        pl.scan_csv(DATA_DIR / "msft_nyt.csv", try_parse_dates=True)
        .with_columns(...)
        .group_by("id_ticker", "year")
        .agg(...)
        .sort("id_ticker", "year"),
        on=["id_ticker", "year"],
        how="left",
        validate="1:1",
    )
    .with_columns(...)
)
```

:::


## Why functions?

Functions let us give names to research-data steps:

- read firm-year data;
- add identifiers;
- summarize articles;
- join supporting data;
- save dated outputs.


## Hands-on

Open `notebooks/1c_polars.ipynb`.
